sentences.text: Yes.
That's right.
I forget the name of the place where this happened in Germany.
But anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the SS had gone underground behind our lines, the local SS people.
And a German communist, a prisoner of war-- not a-- a civilian had come up and told us that-- what he had picked up information.
So we dropped everything else, and were able to get the three head guys, the leaders.
First two, and then the third one, I went to his house several times, and he was never there.
But his house was well stocked, as I remember, with black market supplies that nobody else in Germany [LAUGHS] had.
Anyhow, in the meantime, the other two guys had given us information that they had a cage of arms hidden in the ground not too far from the-- where we-- where this other guy lived.
So we put a few prisoners of war to work starting to dig out.
And then I went back again, and this time I did find the guy.
And I said, I took my-- it wasn't my .45.
It was somebody else's .45.
And I pointed it at him.
And I said, you go 10 feet ahead of me.
I'm not going to give you any instructions where to go.
You know exactly where I want to go.
So he just took-- made a beeline to the cache of arms.
And there we found bazookas, rifles, bullets, dynamite caps, and the Nazi flag that I have there, that I kept because I figured it-- that is mine.
So I told him, now we have all these things here.
You see what we got here.
Now tell me, please, where's the dynamite?
The dynamite caps by themselves are practically harmless.
He said, I don't know.
And since he was not a prisoner of war, because the he was not in uniform, he was a civilian.
I hit him.
And I hit him hard.
And of course, if you have 20 GIs standing around, then there's no particular heroism [LAUGHS] about that.
But I wanted to.
And he said, comrade, don't hit me.
And every time he called me a comrade, [LAUGHS] I hit him again, because he was no comrade of mine.
And he finally said, I'll tell you.
I'll tell you.
And a couple MPs and I went in our jeep.
And he took us to some cave some distance away.
And there was the dynamite.
So.
I really forgot the name of the places.
In Germany.
Pardon?
Oh, it was already behind the Siegfried Line?
No, no.
But that was a very satisfactory [LAUGHS] moment.
After you were done interrogating the prisoners of war, what happened to them?
Where were they taken?
They were usually taken behind the lines into prisoner of war camps.
And they were-- from there on, in bigger units, they were sent back behind the lines.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] So your involvement ended with the interrogation?
That was it.
There's another odd incident.
This was still in Alsace-Lorraine.
We had taken this little village.
And-- excuse me-- and all of a sudden we got word that our tanks had to disperse because the Germans were planning an air attack on our tanks, a special-- excuse me.
And when the people in the village saw that the tanks were pulling back, they thought the whole outfit was pulling back, and they got scared that the Germans were coming back.

And some of them came to us and said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the only person that could given-- had-- could have given the German-- they want the German-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is a fellow here by the name of Hittler.
We thought, they brought Hitler.
[LAUGHS]
So they told us where he lived.
We went there with a couple MPs.
We picked him up, and started to interrogate him.
And the funny thing is every time we called him Herr Hitler, he spelled his name with double T, and he said, but with two Ts please.
[LAUGHS]
Anyhow, we interrogated him, an innocent little fellow.
I just happened to have the name.
So we went to visit his place.
And he was-- said he was a wine ma-- you know, in the wine-- in wine brewery or distilling.
And he had these huge barrels.
And you have seen these huge wine barrels where they locate the-- I mean, keep-- keep the wine.
He said, here, that's what you see.
It turned out the wine barrels were full, full of electronic equipment so [LAUGHS] he could notify the Germans.
Yeah, [LAUGHS] that's what was found.
Well, we said-- you see, we were not trained for that kind of interrogation.

Once we had what we thought was the-- our-- we took him or had him sent right back to division headquarters, and later on even to core headquarters, because that's more of, of another area of interrogation.
But seventh Army-- or our corps got-- got him.
But Herr Hittler was quite a find.
[LAUGHS]
No, these were-- I guess these were the unusual, the somewhat out of [LAUGHS] order.
Yes.
Did you at all stop to talk to anybody?
We talked to them, but since we were on a-- advancing-- we were advanced.
We had to keep up-- keep up with the rest of them.
All we could say-- we gave them maybe C-ration or D, whatever we had.
And-- but we had to keep on going.
And you did not recognize anybody, did you personally?
Oh, no, no, no no.
We--
We were sent to a little place near the spa of Wiesbaden, where they had all the military intelligence personnel gather up.
There were-- I don't know how many-- but a big group of us.
And there I heard that Nuremberg was looking for interpreters and translators and so forth and so on.
And I volunteered again.
And that's how I got to Nuremberg.
It was part of the military, but the military intelligence personnel was kept separate from the rest of the troops.
To keep them together rather, not, not any separation or segregation.
It was just--
Other people did too.
I just forget.
I just told them that I'd be willing to go to Nuremberg.
And that was about it.
I was still in the military, yes.
I was not discharged.
Yeah, the Army transferred me.
Yes.
From Wiesbaden, I don't know.
It's-- it wasn't too far.
But I forget really.
That's--
Strange.
[LAUGHS] The city was a shambles.
And we were quartered in a place they called Boys Town, where the non-commissioned officers [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had their quarters.
And it was called Boys Town.
Boys Town.
Well, a bunch of soldiers and ex-soldiers lived there, and non-commissioned officers.
The lieutenants and above lived in different quarters.
No.
No, nothing.
Nothing.
I thought I was going-- I had hoped I was going to be an interpreter, but I was made a translator.
I tried it out for interpreter once, but I didn't cut the mustard.
[LAUGHS] It's a very difficult thing to hear one language over the earphone, and speak simultaneously into a microphone in another language.
And I, I didn't qualify.
But a funny incident again, one of the interpreters there was a person who had been born in White Russia.
And somehow gotten out of Russia and had lived in England.
And he was one of those people that knew just about every European language that needed to be known.
He spoke English, Russian, of course, Spanish, Italian, German.
And I think a few-- think-- a few other Slavic languages thrown in.
Anyhow, the interpreters only worked every other day.
On their off day, they took a practice session during the court's lunch hour.
They were given difficult legal terms to practice.
In other words, somebody told them something and they had to get it in.
And on this particular day, this person, while talking, while listening Russian, talking English, a friend of his gave him a note written in French.
And while working those two languages in his mind, he answered in writing in French.
It's phenomenal.
Really phenomenal.
I was assigned to a translating section.
And the person in charge there at that time, if I remember right, was Lieutenant [?
Niebergall ?], [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who has since died, but whose wife I met in Wash-- these-- and whose sister-in-law was another secretary lives here in town, Mrs.
Ivy.
And well, they put us to work getting us acquainted.
And, and another person that worked there for a while was the author of the book Also, a, a movie was made of it.
You remember that?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sam-- the name escapes me now, but
And I remember my big supervisor was Colonel Kellogg.
Kellogg, yeah.
He was immediate over the interpretation, over our section.
Photograph, later on photographic evident.
They had the persons examining your pass, your little card.
Yeah, I have it over there.
And the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was about it.
And I never have seen anybody being frisked for weapons.
But I know that one of the reporters-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when-- after a while, security got lacks.
You know how all of the GIs-- put a dog's picture on his ID.
[LAUGHS] And he got in that way.
And then he wrote about it in the papers.
And then all hell broke loose.
No, a GI from the first division.
How do we go?
I think we took a streetcar.
The streetcars were running by that time.
Oh, yes,
Oh, yes.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
In the first trials, the, the Goering, the German government trials.
Well as I've mentioned before, first-- excuse me-- I started out as a translator.
But then photographic evidence needed help in translating the German propaganda film, And after about six weeks of doing that, photographic evidence wanted me to stay on.
They transferred me to photographic evidence.
And there, directly under me, worked Heinrich Hoffmann.
The immediate su-- was it-- Colonel Kellogg.
Yeah, Colonel Kellogg was over the-- all-- over all that section--
--of photographic.
McFadden, I think, was a lieutenant or captain.
No, [INTERPOSING VOICE] above me.
Yeah, you know, you go down the ladder [LAUGHS] or up the ladder.
Well, the other people working in there at that time, we were all of about the same call it category, rank.
And-- but Heinrich Hoffmann worked directly under me.
Heinrich Hoffmann was Hitler's personal photographer.
Well, how would you describe a person when you're-- in the beginning, our relationship was strictly business.
I mean-- He did what we asked him to do, identified persons on pictures in certain situations, and so forth and so on.
But he, personally, did not photograph any of the atrocities.
They were-- he had no-- in his personal files as far of what I saw of it, were no atrocity pictures.
There were all these bigwigs getting together and things like that.
But he had a tremendous knowledge of people in and around Hitler.
And he was a pretty, pretty slick operator.
I mean, he knew how to get around.
But after a while-- and I couldn't give-- couldn't give you the reason-- he and I became friendly on the job.
I never knew even where he lived.
I never visited him or anything.
But on the job, we became very friendly.
And then little by little, he gave me all these photos that I have.
He was, yes.
He was a prisoner of war.
And he-- in fact, he wore, wore American khaki units, because he had no access to his personal belongings.
So US-- the Army or whoever supplied him with the-- American uniforms, if you wish to call it such.
And he was under constant guard.
There was a guard on him.
He couldn't even go to the toilet by himself.
He was under constant guard.
There were other people with us, but somehow I-- I don't know.
We were-- we hit it off.
Let's put it that way.
Whether he had remorse over what had happened, I'm in no position to say.
Well, in the movie that-- in the documentary that-- we talked a lot about Hitler.
And one day, I asked him, you know, it had been rumored that Hitler's sex life was not of the normal kind.
A lot of people had heard about it.
And so one day I asked him.
And he told me that Eva Braun had told him that they had oral sex.
Now Eva Braun used to be Hoffmann's employee.
She worked in his photo lab.
And one day Hitler walked in, and he said to Hoffmann, who's that lady over there?
And Hoffman introduced them to each other.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she became his mistress.
And until a few hours before their death was his mistress, until they were actually married.
And then-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but her married name didn't last but about two hours.
Not, not much really.
Because-- he just told me that he introduced them and--

To get into the courtroom incriminating evidence pictures, like the atrocities that you have seen and you-- and also, for instance, it was-- it so happened we had the lamp shades that Ilse Koch, of-- who was the commander of Buchenwald camp, had made of human skins.
When she saw some better than average looking guys come in, she had them separated right away, had shot them-- had them shot, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] had them skinned, then had the skins tanned.
And of these, then leather, she had made lamp shades.
And I myself carried two of these lamp shades into the courtroom.
It was-- and also, we had to produce organizational charts, anything that you could look at and use as evidence.
I-- they must have come from Buchenwald.
I, personally, do not know.
But since Ilse Koch was camp commander there, they must have come from there.
Well, the Yugoslavs came to us with their pictures for reproduction, of which you saw copies.
They needed reproduction when their case came up.
So the judges got the pictures, and the defense lawyers, and the prosecution so they would know what it was all about, and reproduce it.
And as I said, when the Yugoslav delegation or delegate came in and asked me, can you make us so many copies, I said yes.
How about could I have a copy?
He said, help yourself.
And that's how I got the atrocities in my possession that the Yugoslavs had.
So your responsibility was to bring it into the courtroom proceedings?
Proceedings.
We also presented films.
Now I remember [LAUGHS] another funny thing.
We had a Russian film to show, and somehow I had put the reel backwards.
In other-- it started at the end, or upside down or whatever.
Anyhow, it was wrong.
And with me was one of the Russian guys that was-- and when-- when I showed him-- when the picture started, it started wrong.
And I had to reverse it.
And of course, he couldn't speak English, and I couldn't speak-- but he-- he did like this.
If I would have done that.
[LAUGHS] He said, it would have been my neck.
And the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as I said, organizational charts.
And I remember another thing.
Ohlendorf, I believe it was, a Nazi SS General who was responsible for millions of killings on the Eastern Front, I was seated from him like four or five feet.
The desk on the photo that I have, my desk is just barely visible, I'm not.
And he said, and I heard this, the Final Solution was applied to more than 5 million Jews.
Now, this is an eyewitness statement that I personally heard.
And it struck home.
October [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 16, I believe.
I have it written down there.
[INAUDIBLE]
Kellogg?
Yeah, I think he was a Navy man.
If you have seen the picture about the trials in Nuremberg, the movie-- several years ago they made one-- that was the only thing that was true about it, the courtroom.
[LAUGHS]
